-- Assume for contradiction that the constant function 1 is integrable.
  intro h

  have h₁ : (∫⁻ x : ℝ, ENNReal.ofReal (1 : ℝ)) = ∞ := by

    -- The measure of ℝ is infinite, so the integral is also infinite.
    simp [Real.volume_ℝ]
    <;>
    simp_all [telescope_comap]
    <;>
    simp_all [volume_ne_zero]
    <;>
    norm_num
  -- However, if the function were integrable, this integral would be finite.
  have h₂ : (∫⁻ x : ℝ, ENNReal.ofReal (1 : ℝ)) < ∞ := by
    -- Use the fact that the constant function 1 is integrable to get a finite integral value.
    have h₃ : Integrable (fun _ : ℝ => (1 : ℝ)) := h
    -- Convert the integral to an extended nonnegative real number.
    have h₄ : (∫⁻ x : ℝ, ENNReal.ofReal (1 : ℝ)) = ENNReal.ofReal (∫ x : ℝ, (1 : ℝ)) := by
      simp [h₃]
      <;>
      simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_of_nonneg]
      <;>
      simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_of_nonneg]
      <;>
      simp_all [MeasureTheory.integral_of_nonneg]
      <;>
      norm_num
    -- The integral of the constant function 1 over ℝ is infinite.
    have h₅ : (∫ x : ℝ, (1 : ℝ)) = ∞ := by
      simp_all [Real.volume_ℝ]
      <;>
      simp_all [telescope_comap]
      <;>
      simp_all [volume_ne_zero]
      <;>
      norm_num
    -- Convert the finite integral value to an extended nonnegative real number.
    have h₆ : ENNReal.ofReal (∫ x : ℝ, (1 : ℝ)) < ∞ := by
      simp_all [h₅]
      <;>
      norm_num
    -- Combine the above results to get the contradiction.
    linarith
  -- Contradiction arises because the integral cannot be both finite and infinite.
  linarith
